text: This is a robotic repair of a right indirect inguinal hernia. Somebody emailed me and wanted a completely unedited video here. So again, if it gets a little blurry towards the end, you'll know that it's again, no camera exchanges, no editing other than three X speed, just to kind of move the case along a little bit. But other than that, this is a unedited video.

The surgeon provides a clear view of the medial and lateral leaflets of the peritoneal flap, which converge at the inferior epigastric vessels. The junction is visible as the surgeon crosses over into the supravesicular space on the medial side.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to grasp the hernia sac at an oblique angle, pulling it up through the trocar. The peritoneal flap technique is applied, with cautery used to separate the cord lipoma from the spermatic vessels. The vas deferens is visible, and care is taken to avoid injuring the deferential vessel while continuing dissection.
The surgeon carefully pulls the indirect hernia sac down while focusing on the medial part of the dissection, progressing past the symphysis pubis.
The surgeon adjusts the orientation of the mesh, flipping between a thirty-degree up and down angle to ensure proper placement. High lateral fixation is achieved using two ovicral sutures.

The surgeon ties a granny knot on the pubic tubercle using a Maryland dissector, ensuring high medial fixation. The medial branches of the inferior epigastric vessels are visible, with caution advised due to potential bleeding.
